chunk_summary: Phased therapeutic feeding for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) involves stabilization with F-75, followed by catch-up feeding with F-100, alongside caregiver training and discharge planning to ensure continuity and prevent relapse.
learning_objectives: Understand the phased therapeutic feeding approach for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify key priorities during the stabilization phase, including hypoglycemia and hypothermia prevention, infection treatment, and careful rehydration.
Learn the importance of caregiver training and discharge planning to prevent relapse and ensure continuity of care.
Recognize the role of community nutrition support and structured outpatient follow-up in reducing readmissions.
key_facts: Therapeutic feeding is phased: F-75 is used for stabilization, and F-100 for catch-up feeding.
Stabilization focuses on preventing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infections, and avoiding fluid overload.
Dehydration assessment in SAM is challenging; rehydration must follow protocolized guidance rather than aggressive boluses.
Discharge planning includes caregiver training, danger sign recognition, and follow-up scheduling.
Relapse is common without clear discharge plans, missed infections, or unsustainable household food plans.
Linking discharge counseling with anganwadi monitoring and outpatient reviews improves outcomes.
reference_quote: Therapeutic feeding is phased. Facility protocols use a starter diet such as F-75 to provide maintenance energy while metabolic tolerance is restored, followed by a catch-up diet such as F-100 to drive weight gain once the child is clinically stable and appetite improves. (NHM, 2011)
